title: Kaitiakitanga in Action: Guardianship of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku | Te Mahi Kaitiaki: Te Tiakitanga o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
subject: Social Studies
culturalContext: This lesson is deeply rooted in the history and significance of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (the Mangakōtukutuku Stream), a vital waterway that flows through our rohe. Students will explore how Ngāti Kahungunu ancestors practiced kaitiakitanga through traditional fish weirs (pā tuna) and water management, connecting this to contemporary environmental challenges facing our awa.
learningObjectives: Identify and explain at least three traditional Ngāti Kahungunu practices of kaitiakitanga specific to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku.
Analyze current environmental data to assess the health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku and propose one evidence-based action to address a key issue.
Collaboratively design and present a kaitiakitanga action plan that incorporates both mātauranga Māori and scientific perspectives to enhance the wellbeing of our awa.

activities.title: Whakapapa of the Awa
activities.description: Students begin by learning the whakapapa and pūrākau (stories) of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku from a local kaumātua or kaitiaki. This establishes the cultural and spiritual significance of the awa, framing it as a tupuna (ancestor) and taonga.

activities.title: Wai Māori, Wai Ora Investigation
activities.description: Using water testing kits, students visit the awa to collect and analyze physical and chemical data (e.g., pH, nitrate levels, turbidity). They compare their findings against historical data and cultural indicators of health (e.g., presence of native species like tuna).

activities.title: He Kōrero tawhito: Pā Tuna
activities.description: Students study the design and purpose of traditional pā tuna (eel weirs) through resources and a potential site visit. They create a diagram or model, explaining how this technology demonstrates sustainable harvesting and deep understanding of the ecosystem.

activities.title: Kaitiaki Korero: Community Perspectives
activities.description: In small groups, students prepare and conduct interviews (or listen to pre-recorded interviews) with diverse community stakeholders: a local farmer, a representative from the regional council (Hawke's Bay Regional Council), and a kaitiaki from Mana Whenua. They gather different perspectives on the awa's health and management.

activities.title: Te Mahere Kaitiaki - Kaitiakitanga Plan
activities.description: Synthesizing their learning, student groups develop a specific kaitiakitanga action plan for a section of the awa. Their plan must integrate one traditional practice (e.g., concept of rahui, planting riparian margins with natives) with one modern scientific approach, justifying their choices.

resources: Kaumātua or Kaitiaki from local Ngāti Kahungunu hapū
Hawke's Bay Regional Council 'Land Water Aotearoa' (LWAI) website and water quality data sets
Book: 'Te Mahi Māra Hua Parakore: A Māori Food Sovereignty Handbook' by Jessica Hutchings (for principles of kaitiakitanga)
NIWA's 'Stream Health Monitoring and Assessment Kit' (SHMAK) or similar water testing kits

tasks: Group presentation of their 'Te Mahere Kaitiaki' (Kaitiakitanga Plan) to the class and invited community experts.
Individual written reflection connecting their group's plan to the concept of kaitiakitanga and the specific history of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku.

nzcAlignment: Social Studies L4: Understand how people participate individually and collectively in response to community challenges.
Social Studies L4: Understand how the ways in which leadership of groups is acquired and exercised have consequences for communities and societies.
Social Studies L5: Understand how people's management of resources impacts on environmental and social sustainability.
Tikanga ā-Iwi: Te Ao Māori - Te haere whakamua, te hoki whakamuri | Moving forward into the future, reflecting on the past.
